{{Short description|Natural phenomenon by which all things with mass or energy are brought toward one another}}
{{Infobox physics
| name = Gravity
| image = Apollo 15 feather and hammer drop.ogv
| caption = On the Moon, [[David Scott]] simultaneously dropped a hammer and a feather. Without air resistance, both hit the ground at the same time, demonstrating that gravitational acceleration is independent of mass ([[equivalence principle]]).
| topics = [[Gravitation]], [[Newton's law of universal gravitation]], [[General relativity]], [[Spacetime]]
}}

'''Gravity''' (from Latin ''gravitas'' 'weight'), or '''gravitation''', is a natural phenomenon by which all objects with [[mass]] or [[energy]] — including [[planet]]s, [[star]]s, [[galaxy|galaxies]], and even [[light]] — are brought toward one another. On [[Earth]], gravity gives [[weight]] to physical objects; the [[Moon]]'s gravity causes the ocean [[tide]]s; and the Sun's gravity holds the [[Solar System]] together.

Gravity is the weakest of the four [[fundamental interaction]]s, yet it is the dominant force at macroscopic scales and determines the large-scale structure of the [[universe]].

== History of gravitational theory ==

=== Newton's law ===
{{Main|Newton's law of universal gravitation}}
[[Isaac Newton]] formulated the first mathematical description of gravity (1687):
:<math>F = G\frac{m_1 m_2}{r^2}</math>

=== Einstein's general relativity ===
{{Main|General relativity}}
[[Albert Einstein]]'s [[general relativity]] (1915) reconceptualized gravity not as a [[force]] but as a consequence of the curvature of [[spacetime]] caused by mass and energy:

:<math>R_{\mu\nu} - \frac{1}{2}R g_{\mu\nu} = \frac{8\pi G}{c^4} T_{\mu\nu}</math>

== Properties ==
* Always attractive (in all known cases)
* Infinite range
* Cannot be shielded or neutralized
* Weakest fundamental force (10⁻³⁸ times the strength of the strong interaction)
* Affects all forms of mass and energy, including [[light]]
* Obeys the [[equivalence principle]]: gravitational mass = inertial mass

== Gravitational field ==
The [[gravitational field]] <math>\mathbf{g}</math> at a point is the force per unit mass:
:<math>\mathbf{g} = \frac{\mathbf{F}}{m}</math>

Near Earth's surface: <math>g \approx 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2</math>.

== Gravitational waves ==
{{Main|Gravitational wave}}
In general relativity, accelerating masses produce [[gravitational wave]]s — ripples in spacetime travelling at the [[speed of light]]. First directly detected by [[LIGO]] in 2015 from a binary [[black hole]] merger.

== See also ==
{{Portal|Physics}}
* [[General relativity]]
* [[Gravitation]]
* [[Gravitational wave]]
* [[Newton's law of universal gravitation]]
* [[Spacetime]]
* [[Weight]]

== References ==
{{Reflist|30em}}

[[Category:Gravity| ]]
[[Category:Gravitation]]
[[Category:Fundamental interactions]]
